1.template: Ezekiel the priest, the son of {0}

1.question: Where was Ezekiel when the heavens were opened and he saw visions of God (Ezekiel 1:1)?
options: Among the captives by the river of Chebar

1.question: How many faces and wings did each of the four living creatures have (Ezekiel 1:6)?
According to Ezekiel 1:10, the four faces of each creature were the face of a man, a lion, an ox, and what?
What was beside the living creatures upon the earth in Ezekiel 1:15-16?
options: A wheel, as it were a wheel in the middle of a wheel

1.title: Wheels full of eyes
1.text: The wheels are described as 'so high that they were dreadful' and their rings 'full of eyes round about' — an image of God's all-seeing presence that moves freely in every direction, even among captives far from the temple.

1.question: In Ezekiel 1:24 the noise of the creatures' wings was like what?
options: The noise of great waters, as the voice of the Almighty

1.question: When Ezekiel saw the appearance of the likeness of the glory of the Lord (Ezekiel 1:28), what did he do?

2.question: By what title does the Lord repeatedly address Ezekiel, beginning in Ezekiel 2:1?

2.title: Sent to a rebellious house
2.text: God warns that Israel may not listen, yet sends Ezekiel anyway 'whether they will hear, or whether they will forbear' — the prophet's task is faithfulness, not guaranteed success.

2.template: there was written therein lamentations, and mourning, and {0}

2.question: What was written on the roll of a book shown to Ezekiel (Ezekiel 2:10)?

3.template: and it was in my mouth as {0} for sweetness

3.question: When Ezekiel ate the roll the Lord gave him, how did it taste (Ezekiel 3:3)?
To whom is Ezekiel sent to speak in Ezekiel 3:4-5?
options: To a people of a strange speech and hard language

3.question: What role does the Lord give Ezekiel for the house of Israel in Ezekiel 3:17?

3.title: Seven days astonished
3.text: Before he could speak, Ezekiel sat among the captives at Tel-abib 'astonished' for seven days — overwhelmed by what he had seen before the word of the Lord came to him again.

3.question: Where did the Lord tell Ezekiel to go to speak with him in Ezekiel 3:22?
According to Ezekiel 3:26, what would happen to Ezekiel's tongue?
options: It would be loosed to sing
It would cleave to the roof of his mouth so he would be dumb
It would be burned with a coal

4.template: son of man, take thee a {0}

4.question: What city was Ezekiel told to portray upon the tile in Ezekiel 4:1?
How many days was Ezekiel to lie on his left side, bearing the iniquity of Israel (Ezekiel 4:5)?

4.title: A day for a year
4.text: The Lord tells Ezekiel, 'I have appointed thee each day for a year' (4:6) — the days he lies on each side stand for the years of Israel's and Judah's guilt, turning his body into a living calendar of judgment.

4.question: How was Ezekiel to measure the food he ate during the sign (Ezekiel 4:10)?
options: By weight, twenty shekels a day

4.question: Why would the people of Jerusalem eat bread by weight and drink water by measure (Ezekiel 4:16-17)?
options: Because the Lord would break the staff of bread, so they would consume away for their iniquity
Because of a drought sent by Baal

5.question: What sign act did the Lord command Ezekiel to perform with a sharp knife in Ezekiel 5:1?
options: Shave his head and his beard, then divide the hair

5.question: Why does the Lord say he will not spare Jerusalem in Ezekiel 5:11?
options: Because she defiled his sanctuary with detestable things and abominations

5.title: Set in the midst of the nations
5.text: The Lord says of Jerusalem, 'This is Jerusalem: I have set it in the midst of the nations' (5:5). Chosen to be an example of righteousness to the surrounding peoples, she instead exceeded them in wickedness.

5.question: Which judgments does the Lord say he will send upon Jerusalem in Ezekiel 5:12,17?
options: Famine, evil beasts, pestilence, blood, and the sword

6.question: Against what does the Lord tell Ezekiel to set his face and prophesy in Ezekiel 6:2-3?

6.title: Against the high places
6.text: The 'high places' (6:3) were hilltop shrines where Israel offered to idols. By turning the judgment against the very mountains, the Lord strikes at the heart of the nation's idolatry.

6.question: What did the Lord say the escaped remnant would do among the nations (Ezekiel 6:9-10)?
options: Remember the Lord and loathe themselves for their abominations

6.question: By what three things does Ezekiel 6:11-12 say the house of Israel shall fall?
options: By the sword, the famine, and the pestilence

7.question: What single word does the Lord repeatedly declare has come upon the land of Israel in Ezekiel 7:2-3?

7.title: An end, not just a siege
7.text: Earlier chapters warned of siege; here the message sharpens to a final reckoning — 'An end, the end is come upon the four corners of the land' (7:2). The day of the Lord's wrath has arrived for Israel.

7.question: In Ezekiel 7:10, what is said to have blossomed and budded as the day comes?
According to Ezekiel 7:15, where are the sword, the pestilence, and the famine?
options: The sword is without, and the pestilence and the famine within

7.question: Into whose hands will the Lord give the land for a prey and a spoil in Ezekiel 7:21?
options: The strangers and the wicked of the earth

7.question: According to Ezekiel 7:26, when the people seek a vision of the prophet, what will be lacking?
options: The law from the priest and counsel from the ancients

8.question: To what city was Ezekiel carried in the visions of God in Ezekiel 8:3?

8.title: The image of jealousy
8.text: At the temple gate Ezekiel sees 'the image of jealousy, which provoketh to jealousy' (8:3) — an idol set up in God's own house, the first of a tour of escalating abominations within the sanctuary.

8.question: When Ezekiel dug in the wall as the Lord told him in Ezekiel 8:7-8, what did he find?
How many men of the ancients of Israel stood with censers before the idols, and who was among them (Ezekiel 8:11)?
options: Seventy men, with Jaazaniah the son of Shaphan

8.question: How does the Lord say he will respond to these abominations in Ezekiel 8:18?
options: He will deal in fury; his eye shall not spare, and though they cry he will not hear
He will send a prophet to plead
He will delay for a hundred years

9.template: one man among them was clothed with {0}

9.question: What was the man clothed with linen told to set upon the foreheads of certain men in Ezekiel 9:4?
options: A mark, upon those who sigh and cry for the abominations

9.title: Begin at my sanctuary
9.text: The Lord commands the slayers to spare those who bear the mark but to 'begin at my sanctuary' (9:6) — judgment starts with God's own house and its corrupt leaders before reaching the city.

9.question: What did the man clothed with linen report when he returned in Ezekiel 9:11?
options: That he had done as he was commanded

10.question: What did the Lord tell the man clothed with linen to fill his hands with and scatter over the city (Ezekiel 10:2)?
options: Coals of fire from between the cherubims

10.title: The glory leaves the threshold
10.text: In 10:4 'the glory of the Lord went up from the cherub, and stood over the threshold of the house' — a step in the slow, sorrowful departure of God's presence from the temple before its destruction.

10.question: How are the wheels described in Ezekiel 10:9-10?
options: As a wheel in the midst of a wheel, all of one likeness

10.question: Which four faces did each cherub have, according to Ezekiel 10:14?
As what does Ezekiel identify the cherubims in Ezekiel 10:15,20?
options: The living creatures he had seen by the river of Chebar

11.question: What proverb did the princes of the people say in Ezekiel 11:3?
options: This city is the caldron, and we be the flesh
The fathers have eaten sour grapes
A prophet is not without honour

11.title: Named leaders
11.text: Ezekiel names the princes at the gate — Jaazaniah the son of Azur and Pelatiah the son of Benaiah (11:1) — concrete leaders held responsible for the wicked counsel given in the city.

11.question: What did the Lord say would happen to those who trusted the 'caldron' in Ezekiel 11:8-10?
options: He would bring a sword upon them and judge them in the border of Israel
They would flee to Egypt safely

11.question: In Ezekiel 11:15, what had the inhabitants of Jerusalem said to Ezekiel's brethren in exile?
options: Come back to the city at once
Get you far from the Lord: unto us is this land given in possession

11.question: What new thing does the Lord promise to give in Ezekiel 11:19?
options: One heart and a new spirit, taking away the stony heart and giving a heart of flesh

11.question: Where did the glory of the Lord go and what did Ezekiel then do (Ezekiel 11:23-25)?
options: It filled the temple, and he sang
It went up from the city to the mountain on the east, and he told the captives all the Lord had shown him
It returned to Sinai, and he slept
It vanished, and he wept silently

12.question: What sign act does the Lord command Ezekiel to perform in Ezekiel 12:3-5?
options: Prepare baggage for removing, dig through the wall, and go forth as into captivity

12.title: Eyes that see not
12.text: The Lord describes Israel as a rebellious house 'which have eyes to see, and see not; they have ears to hear, and hear not' (12:2) — so Ezekiel must act out his message in plain sight to break through their blindness.

12.question: Whom does the 'burden' of Ezekiel 12:10 concern?
options: The prince in Jerusalem and all the house of Israel

12.question: What does the Lord say the people will know when he scatters them among the nations (Ezekiel 12:15)?

12.template: eat thy bread with quaking, and drink thy water with {0}

12.question: With what was Ezekiel told to eat his bread and drink his water in Ezekiel 12:18?
options: With quaking and trembling and carefulness

12.question: How does the Lord answer the saying that 'every vision faileth' in Ezekiel 12:23,25,28?
options: He admits the visions were mistaken
He declares that none of his words shall be prolonged, but shall come to pass without delay
He postpones the visions a thousand years
He sends new prophets to Babylon

13.title: Foxes among the ruins
13.text: Verse 4 likens Israel's false prophets to "foxes in the deserts" — scavengers that burrow into broken walls rather than repair them, a vivid charge against leaders who exploit ruin instead of mending it.

13.question: In Ezekiel 13:5, what had the false prophets failed to do?
options: Go up into the gaps and make up the hedge for the house of Israel

13.question: What false message had the prophets given the people, according to Ezekiel 13:10?
options: Peace, when there was no peace

13.template: Say unto them which daub it with {0} mortar

13.question: In Ezekiel 13:16, what did these prophets of Israel see for Jerusalem?
options: Visions of peace, when there was no peace

13.question: What does the Lord promise in Ezekiel 13:23 about the false prophets' power?
options: He will give them new visions
They shall see no more vanity nor divine divinations

14.question: Why do the elders who come to Ezekiel receive no true answer from the Lord (Ezekiel 14:3-4)?
options: They have set up their idols in their heart
They came at the wrong hour

14.question: According to Ezekiel 14:6, what does the Lord command the house of Israel to do?
options: Repent and turn from their idols

14.question: Which three righteous men does Ezekiel 14:14 name, who could deliver only their own souls?
What are the Lord's "four sore judgments" upon Jerusalem in Ezekiel 14:21?
options: The sword, the famine, the noisome beast, and the pestilence

14.title: Daniel among the ancients
14.text: By naming Daniel beside Noah and Job as a byword for righteousness (v. 14), Ezekiel — a contemporary — treats Daniel, then a young exile in Babylon, as already famed for his integrity.

15.title: The useless vine wood
15.text: Unlike oak or cedar, a vine's wood is too crooked and soft to build with; its only worth is its fruit. The Lord's question in verse 2 sets up the whole point: a fruitless vine is good for nothing but burning.

15.question: In Ezekiel 15:4, what becomes of the vine wood once it is cast into the fire?
options: It is shaped into a vessel
The fire devours both ends and the midst is burned
It is made into a pin to hang things on

15.template: And I will make the land {0}

16.question: In the allegory of Ezekiel 16:6, what does the Lord say to the abandoned newborn?

16.template: I clothed thee also with broidered work, and shod thee with {0} skin

16.question: According to Ezekiel 16:14, what made Jerusalem's renown go forth among the nations?
options: Her beauty, made perfect through the Lord's comeliness

16.question: Whom does Ezekiel 16:3 name as Jerusalem's symbolic father and mother?
In Ezekiel 16:27, into whose will did the Lord deliver Jerusalem when she went astray?

16.title: Forgetting the day of rescue
16.text: The repeated charge (vv. 22) is that Jerusalem "hast not remembered the days of thy youth" — the heart of the allegory is ingratitude: forgetting the One who rescued and adorned her.

16.question: In Ezekiel 16:32, to what is unfaithful Jerusalem compared?
options: A wife that taketh strangers instead of her husband

16.question: What does Ezekiel 16:35 call Jerusalem as it summons her to hear the word of the Lord?
According to Ezekiel 16:38, how will the Lord judge Jerusalem?
options: As women that break wedlock and shed blood are judged

16.question: What proverb does Ezekiel 16:44 say will be used against Jerusalem?
options: A prophet has no honor at home
As is the mother, so is her daughter
The fathers have eaten sour grapes

16.question: In Ezekiel 16:46, who are named as Jerusalem's elder and younger sisters?

16.template: Neither hath Samaria committed half of thy {0}

16.question: In Ezekiel 16:49, what does the Lord name as the iniquity of Sodom?
options: Pride, fulness of bread, and abundance of idleness

16.question: Despite the judgment, what does the Lord promise in Ezekiel 16:60?
options: To remember his covenant and establish an everlasting covenant
To rebuild the walls in a day

17.question: In the riddle of Ezekiel 17:3, what does the great eagle take from Lebanon?
options: The highest branch of the cedar

17.question: What did the seed of the land become after it was planted, in Ezekiel 17:6?
options: A spreading vine of low stature

17.template: shall it not utterly wither, when the {0} wind toucheth it

17.question: How does Ezekiel 17:12 interpret the riddle of the first eagle?
options: The king of Babylon coming to Jerusalem and taking its king and princes
The building of a new temple

17.question: According to Ezekiel 17:15, against whom did the vine-king rebel by sending ambassadors to Egypt?

17.title: The tender twig on the high mountain
17.text: After all the judgment, verse 22 turns hopeful: the Lord himself will crop a tender shoot from the high cedar's top and plant it on a high and eminent mountain — long read as a messianic promise that the Lord will raise up the kingdom he chooses.

17.question: What does the Lord declare he does in Ezekiel 17:24, that all the trees of the field may know?
options: He brings down the high tree and exalts the low tree

18.template: the soul that {0}, it shall die

18.question: Of the just man who keeps the Lord's statutes, what does Ezekiel 18:9 declare?
options: He shall be made a king

18.question: In Ezekiel 18:13, what is said of the violent son who does all these abominations?
options: He shall surely die; his blood shall be upon him
He shall live for his father's sake
He shall be made a priest

18.question: According to Ezekiel 18:17, does the righteous son die for the iniquity of his wicked father?
options: He shall not die for it; he shall surely live

18.question: What proverb does the Lord forbid Israel to use, in Ezekiel 18:2-3?
options: A prophet has no honor at home
As is the mother, so is her daughter
The fathers have eaten sour grapes, and the children's teeth are set on edge

18.question: In Ezekiel 18:21, what happens to the wicked who turns from all his sins?
options: He shall surely live; he shall not die
He must first pay a price

18.question: What complaint does Israel raise in Ezekiel 18:25, which the Lord answers?
options: The way of the Lord is not equal

18.title: A new heart and a new spirit
18.text: Verse 31's plea — "make you a new heart and a new spirit" — anticipates the promise of Ezekiel 36 to replace the heart of stone with a heart of flesh; here it is framed as something the people are urged to do.

18.question: How does Ezekiel 18:32 describe the Lord's desire toward the one who dies?
options: He has no pleasure in the death of him that dieth

19.question: In the lamentation of Ezekiel 19:2, to what is the mother of the princes compared?
options: A lioness lying down among lions

19.question: What became of the first young lion, according to Ezekiel 19:4?
options: He was taken in their pit and brought with chains to Egypt

19.template: This is a {0}, and shall be for a lamentation

19.title: From lions to a withered vine
19.text: The poem shifts its figure midway: the princes are first young lions caught by the nations, then a fruitful vine plucked up and dried by the east wind — a double image of Judah's fallen royal house.

20.question: In Ezekiel 20:6, how does the Lord describe the land he swore to bring Israel into?
options: Flowing with milk and honey, the glory of all lands

20.question: What did the Lord give Israel in the wilderness, by which a man should live, in Ezekiel 20:11?
According to Ezekiel 20:21, what did the children do, like their fathers?
options: They rebelled and polluted the sabbaths

20.question: In Ezekiel 20:27, in what further way does the Lord say Israel's fathers had blasphemed him?
options: By committing a trespass against him

20.question: In Ezekiel 20:32, what did Israel say in their mind that shall not come to pass?
options: We will be as the heathen, to serve wood and stone
We will return to the desert
We will choose a new prophet

20.question: What does the Lord say he will do in Ezekiel 20:37 as he gathers his people?
options: Cause them to pass under the rod and bring them into the bond of the covenant
Give them new lands at once

20.title: For my name's sake
20.text: In verse 9 the Lord says he acted "for my name's sake" — a refrain repeated through the chapter — sparing rebellious Israel not because they deserved it, but so his name would not be profaned among the nations.

20.question: What will Israel know when the Lord brings them into the land of Israel, in Ezekiel 20:42?
What does Ezekiel complain people say about him, in Ezekiel 20:49?

21.question: In Ezekiel 21:3, what does the Lord say he will draw forth against the land of Israel?
options: His sword out of his sheath

21.template: to give it into the hand of the {0}

21.question: According to Ezekiel 21:12, against whom will the sword come, that the prophet should smite his thigh?
options: The Lord's people and all the princes of Israel

21.question: How does the king of Babylon use divination at the parting of the way, in Ezekiel 21:21?
options: He makes his arrows bright, consults images, and looks in the liver

21.question: What is commanded of the wicked prince of Israel in Ezekiel 21:26?
options: Remove the diadem and take off the crown

21.title: Until he come whose right it is
21.text: The threefold "I will overturn, overturn, overturn it" (v. 27) ends with "until he come whose right it is" — long read as a messianic promise that the throne would one day rest with its rightful King.

21.question: Against which people is the sword oracle of Ezekiel 21:28 also turned?

22.question: What title does the Lord repeatedly give Jerusalem in Ezekiel 22:2-3?
According to Ezekiel 22:6, who were in Jerusalem, each one to their power, to shed blood?
According to Ezekiel 22:7, how were the fatherless and the widow treated in Jerusalem?
What does the Lord say he will do to Jerusalem in Ezekiel 22:15?
options: Scatter her among the heathen and disperse her in the countries

22.question: To what does Ezekiel 22:18 compare the house of Israel?
options: Dross of silver in the furnace

22.question: How does the Lord describe the land in Ezekiel 22:24?
options: A land flowing with milk and honey
The land that is not cleansed, nor rained upon in the day of indignation

22.question: Whom did the Lord seek in Ezekiel 22:30, to stand in the gap, but found none?
options: A man to make up the hedge and stand in the gap

22.title: Israel as the dross
22.text: Verse 18 turns Israel into the worthless dross left when silver is refined — brass, tin, iron, and lead. The coming siege is pictured as a furnace meant to melt the city down and burn that dross away.

23.question: In the allegory of Ezekiel 23:4, what are the names of the two sisters, and which cities do they stand for?
options: Aholah is Samaria, and Aholibah is Jerusalem
Aholah is Jerusalem, and Aholibah is Samaria
Aholah is Babylon, and Aholibah is Tyre
Aholah is Sodom, and Aholibah is Egypt

23.question: According to Ezekiel 23:11, how did the younger sister Aholibah compare to her sister?
options: She was more corrupt in her doings than her sister

23.question: Whose images, portrayed on the wall, did Aholibah become enamored with, in Ezekiel 23:14?
What happened after the Lord's mind was alienated from Aholibah, in Ezekiel 23:18?
options: His mind was alienated, as it had been from her sister

23.title: Two sisters, one warning
23.text: The names Aholah ("her own tent") and Aholibah ("my tent is in her") hint at the theme: Samaria built her own shrines, while Jerusalem held the Lord's true sanctuary — yet both proved unfaithful.

23.question: Which nations does the Lord say he will raise up against Aholibah, in Ezekiel 23:22-23?
options: The Babylonians, the Chaldeans, and the Assyrians

23.question: What does the Lord say he will set against Aholibah in Ezekiel 23:25?
According to Ezekiel 23:30, why are these things done to her?
options: Because she went after the heathen and was polluted with their idols

23.question: What does the Lord charge Aholibah with in Ezekiel 23:35?
options: She forgot him and cast him behind her back

23.question: What did Aholibah set upon the table before her, in Ezekiel 23:41?
According to Ezekiel 23:42, what did the men from the wilderness put upon the sisters' hands and heads?
What will the sisters know after their judgment, according to Ezekiel 23:49?
options: That I am the Lord God

24.question: What object does the Lord command Ezekiel to set on the fire as a parable, in Ezekiel 24:3?
What does the Lord again call Jerusalem as he announces the great pile for fire, in Ezekiel 24:9?

24.title: The pot whose scum will not come out
24.text: The boiling pot (vv. 3-6) is Jerusalem: its "scum" — its ingrained corruption — clings even as the contents are boiled away, so the Lord finally commands the empty pot itself be set on the coals to burn the filth out.

24.template: Heap on wood, kindle the fire, consume the {0}

24.question: What does the Lord tell Ezekiel he will take away "with a stroke," in Ezekiel 24:16?
How were the people to react to coming grief, following Ezekiel's sign, in Ezekiel 24:23?
options: They should not mourn nor weep, but pine away for their iniquities

24.question: What does the Lord promise about Ezekiel's mouth in Ezekiel 24:27?
options: It shall be opened, and he shall be no more dumb
It shall speak in riddles alone

25.title: A refrain of recognition
25.text: These oracles against the nations keep returning to one purpose: 'they shall know that I am the Lord.' Even judgment on Israel's enemies is meant to reveal who God is.

25.question: In Ezekiel 25:3, what did the Ammonites say 'Aha' against?
options: The Lord's sanctuary, the land of Israel, and the house of Judah

25.question: In Ezekiel 25:8, which two are quoted as saying the house of Judah is 'like unto all the heathen'?
According to Ezekiel 25:15, why is judgment pronounced on the Philistines?
options: Because they have dealt by revenge, with a despiteful heart, for the old hatred

26.question: In Ezekiel 26:3, the Lord says he will bring many nations up against Tyre like what?
options: As the sea causeth his waves to come up

26.question: Whom does Ezekiel 26:7 name as the king the Lord will bring against Tyrus from the north?

26.template: And I will make thee like the top of a {0}

26.question: What does the Lord declare of Tyre in Ezekiel 26:21?
options: She shall be rebuilt in glory
She shall be sought for, yet never be found again
She shall be spared for her wisdom

26.title: A place to spread nets
26.text: The oracle says Tyre will become 'a place for the spreading of nets in the midst of the sea' — the proud mercantile city reduced to a bare rock where fishermen dry their nets.

27.question: In Ezekiel 27:3, what boast does Tyre make of herself?
options: I am the gate of heaven
I am the mistress of kings
I am the strength of the seas

27.question: According to Ezekiel 27:8, the inhabitants of Zidon and Arvad served Tyre as what?
In Ezekiel 27:12, what was Tarshish's trade with Tyre?

27.template: Dedan was thy merchant in precious {0} for chariots

27.question: How does Ezekiel 27:25 describe Tyre at the height of her trade?
options: Replenished, and made very glorious in the midst of the seas

27.question: What broke Tyre in the midst of the seas, according to Ezekiel 27:26?
How do the merchants among the people react to Tyre's fall in Ezekiel 27:36?

27.title: The ship of nations
27.text: The whole chapter is an extended metaphor: Tyre is pictured as a great merchant ship, with timber, sails, oars, and crew drawn from many lands — built to perfection, yet doomed to sink.

28.question: What proud claim does the prince of Tyre make in Ezekiel 28:2?
options: I am a God, I sit in the seat of God
I am the wisest of men
I am the lord of the seas
I am the heir of Eden

28.question: In Ezekiel 28:3, the prince of Tyre is sarcastically called wiser than whom?
In the lamentation of Ezekiel 28:12, how is the king of Tyre described?
options: Full of wisdom, and perfect in beauty

28.question: According to Ezekiel 28:15, the king of Tyre was perfect in his ways until what was found in him?

28.template: Behold, I am against thee, O {0}

28.question: What does the Lord promise Israel in Ezekiel 28:26?
options: They shall dwell safely, build houses, and plant vineyards

28.title: An echo of Eden
28.text: The king of Tyre is said to have been 'in Eden the garden of God,' adorned with precious stones, an 'anointed cherub' — language so cosmic it has long been read as also describing the fall of Lucifer.

29.title: The great dragon of the Nile
29.text: Pharaoh is called 'the great dragon that lieth in the midst of his rivers,' boasting 'My river is mine own.' Egypt's whole wealth flowed from the Nile, so the Lord strikes at the very source of its pride.

29.question: According to Ezekiel 29:6, what had Egypt been to the house of Israel?
How far does Ezekiel 29:10 say the land of Egypt will be made utterly waste?
options: From the tower of Syene even unto the border of Ethiopia
From the desert to the delta

29.question: In Ezekiel 29:14, to which land will the Egyptians be brought back, to be a base kingdom?
In Ezekiel 29:18-19, the Lord gives the land of Egypt to Nebuchadrezzar as wages for his army's labor against whom?

30.question: What does Ezekiel 30:3 announce is near?
options: The day of the Lord, a cloudy day

30.question: When will Egypt know that the Lord is God, according to Ezekiel 30:8?
options: When he has set a fire in Egypt and destroyed all her helpers

30.question: By whose hand will the Lord make the multitude of Egypt to cease (Ezekiel 30:10)?

30.template: And I will set {0} in Egypt

30.question: What does the Lord say he has done to Pharaoh in Ezekiel 30:21?
What recurring result does Ezekiel 30:26 give for scattering the Egyptians?
options: That they shall know that I am the Lord

30.title: Cities by name
30.text: This oracle is unusual for naming so many Egyptian cities — Noph, Zoan, No, Sin, Pi-beseth, Tehaphnehes — pressing home that the judgment would touch the whole land, not just Pharaoh.

31.question: To what does Ezekiel 31:3 compare the Assyrian (Pharaoh's likeness)?
options: A cedar in Lebanon with fair branches

31.template: I have made him {0} by the multitude of his branches

31.question: In Ezekiel 31:10-11, why was the great cedar delivered into the hand of the mighty one of the heathen?
options: Because its roots had dried up
Because its heart was lifted up in its height

31.question: At the end of Ezekiel 31:18, whom does the allegory of the cedar declare it to be?

31.title: Envied by the trees of Eden
31.text: So glorious was the cedar that 'all the trees of Eden, that were in the garden of God, envied him' — yet height became its downfall, a parable that pride goes before destruction.

32.question: In Ezekiel 32:2, to what is Pharaoh compared as he troubles the waters?
options: A young lion of the nations and a whale in the seas

32.question: What does Ezekiel 32:7-8 say the Lord will do to the heavens when he puts Pharaoh out?
options: Cover them and make the stars dark

32.question: Whose sword does Ezekiel 32:11 say shall come upon Egypt?
options: The sword of the king of Babylon

32.question: Which fallen nation does Ezekiel 32:22 name as already lying in the pit, with her graves about her?
Where does Ezekiel 32:32 say Pharaoh shall finally be laid?
options: In the temple of the Nile
In the midst of the uncircumcised, with them slain by the sword

32.title: A descent into the pit
32.text: The second lament leads Pharaoh down into the realm of the dead, where he finds the once-great empires — Asshur, Elam, Meshech and Tubal, Edom — already lying slain. Even the mightiest end the same way.

33.question: According to Ezekiel 33:6, if the watchman sees the sword and blows not the trumpet, what happens?
options: He is rewarded for his silence
The blood of the slain will be required at the watchman's hand

33.template: I have no pleasure in the {0} of the wicked

33.question: In Ezekiel 33:14-15, what must the wicked man do to surely live?
options: Turn from his sin, restore the pledge, and do that which is lawful and right

33.question: What complaint do the children of Israel make in Ezekiel 33:17?
options: The way of the Lord is not equal

33.question: What message did the one who escaped from Jerusalem bring in Ezekiel 33:21?
In Ezekiel 33:24, what do those inhabiting the wastes claim, citing the patriarch Abraham?
options: Abraham was one, and he inherited the land; but we are many
Abraham bought the land from Edom

33.question: How does Ezekiel 33:32 say the people treat the prophet's words?
options: As a very lovely song they hear but do not do
They write them on their walls

33.title: The watchman restored
33.text: Ezekiel is again named a 'watchman unto the house of Israel' (echoing chapter 3). If he warns faithfully, he saves his own soul, whether or not the people listen — duty over results.

34.question: In Ezekiel 34:2, what is the woe pronounced upon the shepherds of Israel?
options: They have lost the flock to Babylon
They feed themselves and not the flocks
They have sold the sheep to Egypt

34.question: What does the Lord declare he himself will do in Ezekiel 34:11?
options: Both search my sheep, and seek them out

34.question: In Ezekiel 34:22, what does the Lord promise to do for his flock?
options: Save his flock so they shall no more be a prey

34.question: Whom does the Lord promise to set up as one shepherd over the flock in Ezekiel 34:23?
How does Ezekiel 34:31 close the shepherd chapter?
options: With a curse on the nations
Declaring 'ye my flock... are men, and I am your God'

34.title: The shepherd who seeks the lost
34.text: 'I will seek that which was lost, and bring again that which was driven away' (v. 16) — this image of God as the shepherd who goes after the straying sheep echoes through Jesus' parable of the lost sheep.

35.question: Against what does Ezekiel 35:2-3 set the prophet's face?
According to Ezekiel 35:5, what does Seir's offense consist of?
options: A perpetual hatred and shedding the blood of Israel in their calamity

35.template: thou shalt be desolate, O mount {0}

35.title: Coveting another's land
35.text: Seir's deeper sin (v. 10) was greed: 'These two nations and these two countries shall be mine.' The land already belonged to the Lord — 'whereas the Lord was there' — so the claim was an affront to God himself.

36.question: To whom is the prophet told to speak at the start of Ezekiel 36 (vv. 1-4)?
What does the Lord promise the mountains of Israel in Ezekiel 36:8-10?
options: They shall sink into the sea
They shall shoot forth branches, be tilled and sown, and be inhabited again
They shall be given to Edom

36.question: In Ezekiel 36:13-14, what accusation against the land does the Lord say will cease?
options: That it devours men and bereaves its nations

36.question: According to Ezekiel 36:20, what did Israel do when they came among the heathen?
options: They profaned the Lord's holy name

36.template: But I had pity for mine {0} name

36.question: What does the Lord promise to give in Ezekiel 36:26?
options: A new law written on stone
A new heart and a new spirit, taking away the stony heart
A new king from the east

36.question: After being cleansed, how does Ezekiel 36:31 say Israel will respond?
options: They will boast of their merit
They will loathe themselves for their iniquities and abominations

36.question: To what does Ezekiel 36:38 compare the repopulated waste cities?
options: The holy flock, as the flock of Jerusalem in her solemn feasts

36.title: Not for your sake
36.text: The Lord stresses that the restoration is 'not for your sakes, O house of Israel, but for mine holy name's sake' — a striking statement that grace flows from God's own character, not from Israel's worthiness.

37.question: Where did the hand of the Lord set Ezekiel down in Ezekiel 37:1?
options: In the midst of a valley full of bones

37.question: In Ezekiel 37, what did the bones become after the breath came into them?

37.template: I shall place you in your own {0}

37.question: In Ezekiel 37:16, what is Ezekiel told to write upon the two sticks?

37.title: Two sticks made one
37.text: The two sticks joined in Ezekiel's hand — one for Judah, one for Joseph — picture the gathering of a divided Israel into a single people, a sign of reunion and restoration.

37.question: In Ezekiel 37:19, the stick of Joseph is said to be in the hand of whom?
According to Ezekiel 37:21, what will the Lord do with the children of Israel?
options: Gather them and bring them into their own land

37.question: In Ezekiel 37:24, who is said to be king and one shepherd over them?

38.question: In Ezekiel 38:2, against whom is Ezekiel told to set his face?

38.title: Gog and Magog
38.text: Gog of the land of Magog became a lasting symbol of the hostile nations gathered against God's people; the New Testament book of Revelation later borrows the names for a final conflict.

38.question: According to Ezekiel 38:11-12, why does Gog plan to attack the land?
options: To take a spoil and a prey from people dwelling safely

38.question: In Ezekiel 38:15, from what direction will Gog come against Israel?
What does the Lord declare he will do to himself in Ezekiel 38:23?
options: Magnify and sanctify himself, and be known to many nations

39.question: According to Ezekiel 39:4, where shall Gog and his bands fall?
In Ezekiel 39:9, for how many years will the people burn the weapons with fire?

39.title: Seven months of burying
39.text: So great will be the fall of Gog's multitude that Israel will spend seven months burying the dead to cleanse the land, and the burial place will be called the valley of Hamon-gog.

39.question: In Ezekiel 39:11, what name is given to the place where Gog is buried?

39.template: the name of the city shall be {0}

39.question: According to Ezekiel 39:23, why did the house of Israel go into captivity?
options: For a famine in the land
For their iniquity, because they trespassed against the Lord

39.question: What does the Lord promise to pour out upon the house of Israel in Ezekiel 39:29?

40.title: The man with the measuring reed
40.text: Ezekiel sees a man whose appearance is like brass, holding a line of flax and a measuring reed; throughout chapters 40-42 this guide measures every part of the new temple before Ezekiel's eyes.

40.question: In Ezekiel 40:3, what did the man whose appearance was like brass hold in his hand?
options: A line of flax and a measuring reed

40.question: In Ezekiel 40:6, toward which direction did the gate the man came to look?
According to Ezekiel 40:16, what was carved upon the posts of the gate?
What was repeatedly being done to the gates and courts throughout Ezekiel 40?

40.template: he measured from gate to gate toward the south an hundred {0}

40.question: In Ezekiel 40, by what unit are the gates and courts measured?
According to Ezekiel 40:39, what stood in the porch of the gate, on each side?
options: Two tables to slay the offerings on

40.question: In Ezekiel 40:44, whose chambers were by the inner gate?
According to Ezekiel 40:49, the porch was reached how?
options: By the steps whereby they went up to it

41.question: In Ezekiel 41:4, what did the man call the inner room he measured?

41.title: Measuring the temple itself
41.text: Having measured the outer gates and courts, the guide now turns to the temple proper — its posts, doorways, and the inner room called the most holy place.

41.question: According to Ezekiel 41:5-8, what surrounded the house on every side?
Toward which direction did the face of the house and the separate place look in Ezekiel 41:14?
In Ezekiel 41:18-19, what two figures were carved alternately on the walls?

41.template: the face of a {0} was toward the palm tree on the one side

41.question: According to Ezekiel 41:25-26, what was carved on the doors of the temple, like on the walls?

42.title: The priests' chambers
42.text: After measuring the temple, Ezekiel's guide turns to the priests' chambers beside the 'separate place' — the open area that walled the holy sanctuary off from the rest of the court.

42.question: In Ezekiel 42:5, why were the upper chambers shorter than the lower ones?
options: The galleries took up more space from them

42.question: According to Ezekiel 42:3-6, in how many stories were the chambers built?
In Ezekiel 42:13, what does the man say the north and south chambers are?
options: Holy chambers where the priests eat the most holy things

42.template: to make a separation between the {0} and the profane place

43.title: The glory returns
43.text: Years before, Ezekiel had watched the glory of God leave the Jerusalem temple. Now the glory of the God of Israel returns by the east gate, with a voice like many waters, and fills the house once more.

43.question: In Ezekiel 43:2-5, by which way did the glory of the God of Israel come into the temple?
options: From the way of the west
From the way of the east

43.question: In Ezekiel 43:7, what does the Lord call the temple?
options: The place of my throne, where I will dwell forever
A place of rest for the kings

43.template: the altar shall be twelve cubits long, twelve broad, {0} in the four squares thereof

43.question: According to Ezekiel 43:18-21, what was the altar to be used for?
options: To offer burnt offerings and sprinkle blood thereon
As a seat for the prince

43.question: In Ezekiel 43:27, what does the Lord promise once the altar is consecrated?

44.question: In Ezekiel 44:2, why is the east gate of the sanctuary to remain shut?
options: Because the Lord, the God of Israel, has entered in by it

44.title: A gate for the prince
44.text: Though the east gate stays shut, verse 3 makes one exception: the prince may sit in it to eat bread before the Lord, entering and leaving by the porch of that gate.

44.question: According to Ezekiel 44:10, what shall the Levites who went astray after idols bear?
In Ezekiel 44:15, which priests may come near to minister to the Lord?
According to Ezekiel 44:17-18, what must the priests wear when they minister in the inner court?

44.template: they shall teach my people the difference between the {0} and profane

44.question: According to Ezekiel 44:21, what may no priest do when he enters the inner court?
In Ezekiel 44:28, what does the Lord say will be the inheritance of the priests?
options: A portion of land in every tribe
The Lord himself ('I am their inheritance')

45.question: In Ezekiel 45:1-4, the holy portion of the land is set apart for whom and for what?
options: For the merchants and their trade
For the priests and the sanctuary
For the army and its camps

45.title: Just balances
45.text: Right after commanding the princes to put away violence and spoil, the Lord demands 'just balances, and a just ephah, and a just bath' — honest weights and measures in everyday trade.

45.question: According to Ezekiel 45:9, what does the Lord command the princes of Israel to do?
options: Remove violence and spoil, and execute judgment and justice

45.question: In Ezekiel 45:18, on the first day of the first month, what is to be done?
options: Take a young bullock and cleanse the sanctuary

45.question: According to Ezekiel 45:21, what feast is to be kept in the first month, on the fourteenth day?

46.question: In Ezekiel 46:1, when is the east gate of the inner court to be opened?
options: On the sabbath and the new moon

46.title: Enter one gate, leave by another
46.text: Verse 9 sets an orderly rule for the feasts: whoever comes in by the north gate must go out by the south, and whoever enters by the south goes out by the north — no one turns back the way he came.

46.question: According to Ezekiel 46:9, at the solemn feasts, how must a worshipper leave the temple?
options: By the same gate he entered
By the gate opposite the one he entered

46.question: In Ezekiel 46:10, where is the prince when the people go in and out?
According to Ezekiel 46:16-17, a gift the prince gives to a servant returns to the prince in what year?

46.template: where the {0} of the house shall boil the sacrifice of the people

47.title: The river from the temple
47.text: From under the threshold of the temple flow waters that grow ever deeper — a healing, life-giving river. The image echoes again in Revelation 22, where a river of living water flows from the throne of God.

47.question: In Ezekiel 47:3-4, as the man measured, how did the waters rise?
options: From the ankles, to the knees, to the loins

47.question: By Ezekiel 47:5, what had the waters become?
options: A river that could not be passed over

47.question: According to Ezekiel 47:9, what happens wherever the river comes?
In Ezekiel 47:10, who shall stand by the river from En-gedi to En-eglaim?

47.template: This shall be the border, whereby ye shall inherit the land according to the twelve {0} of Israel

47.question: According to Ezekiel 47:15-17, the borders being described mark out what?
In Ezekiel 47:22-23, what does the Lord say about the strangers who sojourn in Israel?
options: They shall have inheritance among the tribes of Israel
They shall not enter the land

48.question: What is being divided among the named tribes throughout Ezekiel 48?
options: The land, in portions for each tribe

48.question: In Ezekiel 48:10, the holy oblation is set apart for whom, with the sanctuary in its midst?
According to Ezekiel 48:15, the five thousand left over is to be a profane place for what?
options: The city, for dwelling and suburbs

48.question: In Ezekiel 48:19, those who serve the city are to be from where?
options: From all the tribes of Israel

48.question: According to Ezekiel 48:21, where is the sanctuary of the house set, in the prince's portion?

48.title: The sanctuary at the center
48.text: In the apportioning of the land, the sanctuary of the house is set in the midst of the holy oblation — a deliberate picture of God's presence at the very center of the restored nation.

48.question: Through Ezekiel 48:23-28, what is each of the remaining tribes (Benjamin, Simeon, and others) given?

48.template: the name of the city from that day shall be, The {0} is there
